CUBRRENT AFFAIRS,

‘We see by a programme that is before us that “ Mem-,
bers of the Higher Line’”’ of Stonyhurst College, Eng-
land, where many a Bung Esquire sends Master Bun,
for “tone’’ and an accent, played “The Shaughraun
recently. A Master Osborne Ryan: took the part .of
« Corry Kinchela,”” and a Master Bernard Cinnamon
interpreted—no doubt in a spicy manner—*Con, ‘the
Shaughraun, the soul of every fair, the life of every
funeral, - the first fiddle at all weddings.”” Might wo
suggest to the highly r(j,spect?ble” authorities of
Stonyhurst that if they wsll deign to touch Ireland
with a forty-foot pole, that some other character be-
gides “ the life of every funeral,”” might be the point of
contact.  Might we suggest to the authorities of

Stonyhurst College that death is a lonesome and a sad
thing, and that funerals are solemn affairs; also that
they must not judge Ireland by the wastrels of imitation
« respectability,”’ that ignorant and upstart parents
export over to their College. &

The Tipperary (Barronstown) Races aro to be held
~n the 29th and 30th May. At a meoting of the Race
Committee an application was received f‘rom the Charles
Kickham brass band of Tipperary, with reference to
gﬁpplying the music for the occasions. Previously, we

understand, a Tommy Atkins’ band invariably graced
these races in “ gallant ’’ Tipperary. The members of
the Committee, with the exception of four, were in
favour of giving the order to the civilian band, which,
we hear, is an excellent band, and quite equal to any
military one. The owner of the course, it is said, ob-
jected to the civilian band being employed, and the
supporters of the Kickham band, though in the ma-
jority, gave way. There’s Gallant’’ Tipperary for
you. But what of the usual contributors to the funds
for these races when the Committee send out their
appeal for subscriptions? After all they—if they ob-
ject to a civilian band being brushed aside for a Tommy
Atkins’ one—have a legal right to keep their money in
their pockets. Perhaps some of them may think it a
patriotic duty to do so.

The lads of Cork are not to be wheedled into the
British Navy, even by a training ship at Queenstown,
and the Government threaten to remove the ship! No
threat of that kind will intimidate National Ireland in
the least, whatever effect it may have on the Bungs who
fill the sailors with refreshment.”” We were pleased
to read that there were only twenty-five Irish boys on
board the Queenstown training ship; it is far better
for Trish boys to be engaging in industrial work that
adds to the strength and wealth of the Irish Nation.
The Bungs and others of Kingstown are anxious to
have a training ship, or even a guard ship, in their
harbour; no doubt Bung thinks that, notwithstanding
the “Bona’’ traffic that disgraces Sundays, that the
volume of thirst is not as great as it might be. The
patriotic Kingstown Urban Council sent over a depu-
tation. Mr. A. E. Mills, a Kingstown District Coun-
cillor, suggested to the First Lord of the Admiralty
that if there was a question of removal of the Queens-
town training ship, which it had been said was not a
success, the Admiralty might try the experiment of
placing it for an experimental period at Kingstown.
If the authorities would remove all their soldiers’ bar-
racks as well as their training ship out of the country
it would be a good riddance.

Who comprise “The Irish Branch of the British
Dental Association?’ Whoever they are, they had a
dinner at Cork last Saturday. The first toast was
“The King,”” a toast which has been turned into a
party cry in this country. However, in toasting that
party cry the members of the Irish branch of the
British Dental Association are no worse than the mem-
bers, including many of the staff of the Freeman, of the
Irish tail of the British Institute of Journalists.

We see that Buff, the evening paper from the vat of
Lord Bung (Ardilaun), is again pecking at Mr Murphy’s
Green. It is all over the question of supplying the
betting mugs, that are a pest in this country, with the
earliest information about some English horse races!
Buff refers to Green’s “‘ Waterbury’ Brigade.”” We
aro sure that thero must be some keen satire hidden
away in that phrase, though we rank outsiders don’t
see it. In the manner in which a London cabby abuses
a-London ’bus driver afteér a collision, Lord Ardilaun’s
Buff shouts:— But the Herald is like the negro’s
dog, ‘which would have won ‘if it hadn’t been for the
other dog.” The Masl, which did not brag about.its
money or its brains, or endeavour to raise its credit by.
quoting the achievements of other papers, beat this
performance of the Herald hollow.” May we again
suggest that the Mail Billingsgate writer should meet
the Herald's sporting tipster and have it out, Pink's
““prophet ”* being referee. The “scrap ''—we believe:
that is the technical term—might take place, by pez
mission of the police, on' O’Connell Bridge,.at three
o’clock some evening. If the “scrap’’ comes: off we
should say it would %e two to one on Trollypole.

At a “grand ”’ concert, held in the Mansion House,
one evening last week, there were songs in four lan-
guages, but the Irish language was utterly ignored.
The concert was in aid of St. Peter’s National Schools,
‘Whitefriar Street. We feel sure that his Grace the
Archbishop of Dublin, who interests himself so keenly
in the Irish language movement, must have felt any-
thing but pleased that the concert was held under his
patronage; no doubt he was utterly ignorant of the
nature of the programme until one was placed in his
hands at the concert. The second item was a song
“Quando le sere al placido;’’ the third item—Cavatina
—“Plus grand dans son obscurité;’’ the seventh—
Reverie— Sognai;’’ the fourth item in Part II. was
song—* Prologue (I. Pagliacci);”’ the oighth—Ro-
manza— Alla Stella Confidente.”” The whole affair
wound' up with the duet “Still wie die Nacht.” In
what ‘part of Venice or Paris or Berlin is Whitefriar
Street; where the St. Peter’s National Schools are?

In: the annual report of the Scripture Readers’ and
Irish Society we read:— The mission vans worked as
usual . through the summer scason, visiting several dis-
tricts; In one case a number of T.C.D. students had
accompanied the van as colporteurs, and had done
splendid work.” ~We wonder were there any mud-
throwing students amongst this lot, and did they all
know how to spell M-a-y-o-r—Mayor? Also, are these
particular T.C.D. students shedding the light on the
poor Idolatrous obscurants who tramp the halls of
Trinity as Nutting scholars!

The Archbishops and Bishops of the Church of Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland have passed a reso-
lution re the proposed English Education Act. The
resolution contains three paragraphs; this is the
second : —*‘ Enjoying as we do in Ireland the priceless
blessing of freedom to convey to our children in our
own schools the fullest religious instruction in accor-
dance with our formularies, and being aware that the
same freedom is claimed and valued by all other re-
ligious denominations in this island, we are unable to
understand the conscientious objection to such teaching
professed by Nonconformists in England.”’” The italics
are owrs. Quite so; the “Saved ’’ enjoy, the “Idola-
tors,”” to a great extent, have to be satisfied with
“claiming *’ and “ valuing.”” * Whilst the “ Saved’’ have
Trinity College, where the spelling experts and the
mud-throwers come from, the “Idolators’’ are free to
keep on “claiming’’ their University rights.

We learn from the statement made by the Provost
(Bigot Traill), of the University of spelling and medical
fame, at the ““ General Synod > of the Church of Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland, that the total
Clerical payments from both sources, annuities and
stipends, were £290,087 in 1904, and £290,019 in
1905. Is not this enough to shock the most hardened
“economic sense?’”” In round figures £300,000 spent
on non-productive Church of England’s Faithful Gar-
risori “Clergymen! What industries could be started,
whatibraries founded, what Universities kept going,
what drainage works completed, for half that sum! No
wonder this country is backward.  £300,000 spent
anm'li&&‘lly on non-productive parsons, and this does not
include, we presume, collections at the doors and on the
plates.

At a meoting of a corporation by the namo of the
“ Church Education Society,”” held in Molesworth ]E[al},
the Rev. Canon O’Connor (“Saved’’) conclugled h}s
speech, according to Lord Bung’s Zapress, in this
way :—“ Romanism, he went on to show, st_ood for
nothing except the dark ages, while Protestantism, the
world over, was synonymous with progress and en-
lightenment.”” And Lord Bung’s Eapress neglected to
place three notes of exclamation after that. For real
enl‘ight‘enmont 20 to the Potteries, or some such place,
in “Saved ”’ England.

At the annual meeting of the ‘ Open-air Mission for
Treland,”” of which the notorious Mr. Hallowes, of Ark-
low, is apparently a leading light, a letter was read

THE LEADER.

- May 5, 1906.

from the Rev. J. O. Gago Dougherty, who stated that
ho oxpected next Sunday to receiwe a convert from
Romanism in his own church, whose conversion he
attributed to the direct results of open-air mission wm_-k_
We wonder do they give penny rolls and pieces of hairy
bacon away at some of these open-air gatherings. The
fact of one solitary convert ”’ being “ expected *’ would

lead ono to suspect things.

tho same meoting, the Rev. William Harpur, ac-
corﬁgng to Bung’s E’zpr;'css, referred to the great field of
mission work to bo found throughout _bhp year at fairs
and markets, when members of the mission had an op-
portunity of speaking, to the country people. “The
more one saw of the good which coulfl be accomplished
in this way, the more one became convinced of the neces-
sity of maintaining and extending the work, especially
when it was remembered that the strect: preachers had
often at hand the only means by which many poor
peoplo could be reached who were thirsting for the Word

of God.”

Tt is characteristic how the so-called religion of these
anti-Irish preachers is founded upon hate of Catholics.
Hate of Catholics appears to be their chief motive
power. No wonder the Soupers and the Scripture readers
since the time of “Mick M‘Quaid”’ to the present—
have been ludicrous failures. ~ You cannot construct
anything on a foundation of hate—and so the Pervert
Priests’ Protectors and the like foam at the mouth and
gesticulate, and the people with simple faith and love
in their hearts pass along not heeding the hysteric
haters of Catholicity. However, these preachers gather
in a lot of money from foolish old women, and so they
manage to have a soft time of it in this world.

The Local Government Board auditor has issued his
report concerning the accounts of that home of Bigotry
—the Rathmines Urban District Council—that this
year added 1s. 9d. in the £ to the rates that were pre-
viously paid by the unfortunate ratepayers of that dis-
trict. The Report concerns the year ending March 31st,
1905, and so it deals with accounts upwards of a year
old. The auditor says:—The wages books were not
duly or regularly certified during the year just audited,
and T asked the clerk to arrange with the surveyor for
the proper certifying of these accounts in future.”” We
trust that the clerk, a Mr. Fawsett, will see that this
does not occur again. He is a very highly-paid official,
and it is poor consolation to the unfortunate ratepayers
that the wages books were not duly or regularly certi-
fied during the year.

The auditor goes on to make the following severe re-
marks:—“T regret to have to report that the Collector
of Rents of Working Class Houses, Michael J. Chris-
topher, was unable to account satisfactorily for dis-
crepancies in his books, and, after prolonged inquiry
into all the circumstances, I surcharged him with a
total sum of £37 14s. 6d., which I found had been lost
to the Council through his misconduct or negligence.’’

Tho L.G.B. auditor proceeds to deal at length with
this matter, as it is ““a serious as well as a complicated
one.”” He points out that the keeping of the accounts
for rents seems to have been wholly left to the collector,
and that there is no check on the termination of the
tenancies. ~ Ho says:—“He (the collector) further
states that it never, even in the case of a short tenancy
of a few W'ecks, occurred to him until T pointed it out
that allowing a tenant who only paid four weeks’ rent
to occupy a house for five weeks involved a loss to the
Council.” Evidently this Rathmines collector does not
know how many beans make five; but the ratepayers
have to pay an extra 1s. 9d. in the £ this year.

On next Sunday, May 6th, a Gaelic tournament will
take 'pluce at Cashel in aid of the local Christian Bro-
thers” Schools. The tournament is under the patronage
of his Grace the Archbishop of Cashel.

The Galway Orchestral Society 'gave a “ grand *’ con-
cert recently. Of course, there was a “coon’’ song, 4

v

g8,

and, of course, there was 70t a song in Irish on the pro-
gramme. There was one dance, and that was a
“Spanish Fan Dance.”  Alas! poor Galway. Yet
with all your coony tone you still are waiting for some-
thing to turn up.

The Rev. Mr. Hannay, the Bigot of Westport, is the
subject of a vigorous but temperate letter from the pen
of Father McDonald, the Administrator of Westport,
which appears in our contemporary the Mayo News.
The Rev. Mr. James Oliver Hannay, Protestant Rector
of Westport, Master of Arts of Trinity College, Dublin,
and a native of Belfast, is the person who writes
travesties under the name of “ George A. Birmingham.”
Father McDonald would not readily believe that
“ George A. Birmingham’’ was a pen name of the
Rev. Mr. Hannay:—

I could not be convinced of that, writes Father McDonald, be-
cause I regarded the rector of Westport as a cultured gentleman,
and no bigot, and because the books in question contain gross
libels on living men, and virulent attacks on bishops, priests, and
nuns. I resolved, when next I should meet the rector, to put him
the question and get an answer from himself direct. I have put
the question, and to my astonishment and regret, he confessed he
is the author. So we must not any longer be attributing the libels
and the bigotry in these books to the unknown and colourless “Mr.
Birmingham,” but to their real author, Rev. James Oliver Hannay,
Iﬂ ,:Iasler of Arts of Trinity College, Dublin, and a native of Bel-
ast.

Father McDonald goes on:—

The books pretend to describe Irish life as it is at the present
day, but any intelligent reader will lay them down with the con-
viction that they are chiefly an attack on the position and influence
of the Catholic Church in Ireland. It is true that he feigns to
conceal the identity of his characters; but anyone with a scrap of
local knowledge will see through the thin veil, and will be forced
to conclude that the author wished the originals to be easily iden-
tified. Such a plan has two obvious effects. It creates a sale for
the books; and it enables an unscrupulous writer first to describe
his characters so that the reader will readily recognise them, and
then to asperse and libel them without the risk of mentioning
names. I shall illustrate this from the Rev. Rector’s latest book.
He describes Capt. Quinn as a “slim small man” from the County
of Mayo, who goes out to South Africa at the head of a small
band of Irish volunteers to help the Boers in their struggle against
England; and, when the war is over, his friends and admirers get
him nominated to represent, in the British Parliament, a division
of his native County of Mayo. From such a description there is
not in all Connaught a boy over ten years of age who would not
lay his finger on the man. No one could be more couscious of
that fact than tha rev. author; and still he proceeds to libel the
captain in a fashion that makes one sad to know that the words
were written by a Christian minister. “There were strange lines,”
he writes, “absut his cheeks and jaws which somehow suggested
that the man had seen a good deal of the evils of the world, and
rot altogether unwillingly.” So vile were the Captain’s sugges-
tions that a man felt the meaner for having sat near him in the
same room; and so encompassed was he with the network of his
own crimes that he was glad, when the war broke out, to escape
to South Africa on the pretence of fighting for the Boers.
Now, sir, to bring home the baseness of such a description, suppose
I said that there is a Protestant Rector in a certain town in Mayo,
a prominent member of the Gaelic Loague and the author of two
novels, every person in the county would know that man. And
suppose, after Mr. Hannay’s own style, I then went on to say that
this rector never pays his just debts, and, though a married man,
he is known to have criminal intimacy with females of his own
flock, 1 should feel, if I had done so, that I had made myself an
outcast. I should expect the condemnation of every man in whom
there was a vostige of principle or honour. And I should hide my-
self some place where my infamy was unknown.

Father McDonald proceeds to deal with Mr. Han-
nay’s observations on Westport, which, in that frank
and valiant spirit that is so characteristic of him, the
“ novelist  disguises as “ Clogher.” Father McDonald,
after quoting from the valiant “George A. Birming-

ham’s’’ book, remarks:—

Anybody who knows Westport and reads Mr. Hannay's descrip-
tion of Clogher will have little dl(ﬁgully in admitling their iden-
tity. Ho will observe that Clogher is a town in tho westorn sca,
that the bay close by has hundreda'nf groen xaln.nda,' L]n_zb from the
railway station to the town there is a trot down hill into a long
streot which runs straight to the gates of the demesne. Ho will
observe also that the great house in tho demesno has two large
eagles perched on its corners, that it stands within a fow yards of
a wide artificial lake, and, more important than all, that the town
of Clogher has a présbytery with an administrator and two curates.
The rov. suthor made sure that the reader could have no doubt
about the place. It was & necessary preliminary for thg work ho
had set himself to do. And having dono that part of it, he pro-
oceds to state that tho Roman Catholic Administrator of Clogher
reccives through the landlord’s agent £100 o year. When a now

~ landlord came over {0 estata he enquirad ebout the c.use for

THE LEADER.

163

giving this money to the priest, but the agent quickly satisfied him
by saying, ““It is worth while paying it, because it gives us a sort
of hold ovor Fathor Fahy. As long as it is paid things won't get
much beyond tho talking stage here. The cstate will be easily
managoed. The money is, in fact, a bribo to the priest to keep tho
people quict. IF the rest of the gentry had done as your family
did, there mever would have been a land agitation. The priests
would have been our most valuable allies.” Now, sir, I charac-
terise that as tho most audacious lio that cver appeared in print,
and I characterise its author as a disgrace to his church.

Father McDonald concludes in this way:—

1 havo been for six years administrator of Westport, and a
shilling of Lord Sligo’s money I have never seen. And my pre-
docessor, the presont Parish Priest of Louisburgh, can state the
same.  When I heard from the man himself that he was the author
of theso books, I asked him what justification he had for such a
calumny on the administrator of Westport. The only reply he
could mako was that *Clogher is not necessarily Westport”’—a
characteristic reply, ovasive and miserable! What I said to him
then I repcat here now, “Whether Clogher is Westport or not, I
am an Irish priest, and'T challenge the Rev. Mr. Hannay to point
out to mo any priest within the four seas of Ireland who accepts
such a bribe.”

Tll suy nothing, for the present, of the difference it would have
mado if the author had published these vile charges over his own
name. For then the public would know with whom they were
conversing, and would, from long experience, be able to make due
allowance for the traditional bigotry of his class.

Perhaps the only comment that we need make on all
this is that the Rev. Mr. Hannay is a Master of Arts
of Trinity College, Dublin.

The Annual Report of the Committee of the City and
County of Cork Bungs contains a request that they, the
Bungs’. Committee, “ feel it their duty to again call the
attention of the trade to the importance of giving the
preference to goods of Irish manufacture.” We, in
turn, feel it our dubty to call the attention of the
patriotic Cork Bungs’ Committee to the fact that the
water-mark on the cover of this Report is “ Glen-
burnie,”” a word with an unmistakable Scotch flavour,
and the water-mark on the inside paper of the Report
is “ Pioneer Fine.”’

In the Report of the Cork Bungs, we read:—“Tt is
perfectly absurd to say that because of the indiscre-
tions of a small number the vast bulk of the population
who use drink without abusing it should be deprived of
the ordinary facilities for obtaining refreshment.”
This has reference to the attempt to fittingly honour
St. Patrick by shutting up the drink shops, and giving
Bung’s Bond Slaves a well-deserved holiday on that day

These Cork Bungs also declare:—“The Saturday
Early Closing Bill was again introduced. It was second
order for Friday, the 7th April, and its promoters had
every hope that it would be reached. They were, how-
every, disappointed, owing to the action of your Com-
mittee, and the chance for its ever being considered
during the session was practically destroyed. Notwith-
sanding this, the Bill was kept down, first from weelk to
week, and then from day to day, almost to the close of
the session, in the hope of slipping it through the
second reading stage.””  Amongst the subscribers to
this Cork Bungs’ Organisation we see Brother Gould-
ing’s firm, Kiloh and Co., is down for a guinea.

The Limerick Industrial Association has not let the
Van Eesbeck cap affair end without corresponding with
Christy’s, of London. The Limerick Industrial Asso-
ciation wrote briefly the facts of the case. Churistys re-
plied : —“In reply to your letter of the 6th inst., we
have looked into the matter in' question, and regret to
find the labels referred to were put in the caps by us
according to Mr. Van Eesbeck’s directions. It appears
the order was taken by our representative, and by an
oversight the instructions were unfortunately allowed
to pass, which would not have occurred had the order
been addressed direct to the house. In fact, on a
former order we absolutely ignored instructions. We
deeply regret the occurrence, and assure you it will not
occur again.”’ In this letter Christys admit that they
marked caps made by themselves as Irish-made! We
need not concern ourselves with their regret, whatever
its depth may be, now that they have been found out!
When we find a British manufacturing house like
Christys doing this sort of -business, the fact indicates

how crying is the need for a proper policing of the goods
that are fraudulently palmed off as Irish manufacture.

We are glad to see that the projected Irish boot
factory on American lines is exciting a lively interest.
The financial difficulty, in view of the successful flota-
tion of the Kilkenny Woollen Mills, ought to be easily
overcome. The chief difficulty at present is that those
interested in the project require promises of ocapital
before they proceed to registration and issue a pros-
pectus. Mr. William Hayden of 9 Eden Quay, who
is working up the matter, will, we assume, as soon as
he feels that the proferred support warrants it, call a
meeting of those interested, and consider the fox"mai:ion
of a businesslike Board of Directors and issue a pros-
pectus. We were not surprised that Mr. Hayden re-
ceived a promise of support during tho week from a
Maynooth man well known to our readers and well
known to the police,Domnaill us Duacalta. It was
only last week thatMac ua Duacattahad a letter in

Irish, and in Irish only, on his cant. Needless to say
the letter to Mr. Hayden was in Irish. As it concerns
a public project, our readers may be interested in it.

\ A SUGGESTION FOR A NEW DICTIONARY.Z

DICTIONARY does not, as a general rule, call
A up visions of flowery, mental pasturage. = We
rather think of it as a sort ‘of cemetory where words lie
dead, with their family extraction hinted by way of
etymology, and their virtues and duties, when they
wero alive, given in the tombstony form of definitions.
Indeed, not to mince matters too small, we are apt to
think of the common, ordinary dictionary as a very
dry thing, indeed; a sort of Golgotha where the bones
of language lie bleaching in the sandy, waterless desert
of oblivion. True, the great Oxford dictionary, still so
far from completion, is anything but dull; a man might,
in a sense, educate himself by poring over its pages.
But I am not thinking of Dr. Murray’s mighty work,
but rather of those helpful and succulent manuals so
common in the middle of the last century, published at
a cheap rate (they were dear, at any rate!) as “ John-
son’s Pronouncing Dictionary.”” This was a manifest
taking of the doctor’s name in vain, for there was not
a feature about them that recalled the original John-
son’s Dictionary, a distinctly interesting and able work,
for its time. I am rather thinking of those stimulating
works in which you found, say, the narcissus, described
with a noble comprehensiveness as “a kind of flower,”
or the ptarmigan (if that feathered beast happened to
be mentioned at all) as “a kind of bird,”” or the stur-
geon vividly set down as “a sort of large fish.”” Tt is
not for the purpose of enlarging on the special shortcom-
ings of these once common works that I refer to them,
but rather with the object of making it clear that the
kind of dictionary I mean to write about in this article
is not of the “ Pronouncing ’’ kind, but of the descrip-
tive kind; and that if it were reasonably well done it
would certainly prove itself to be not, in the least, repul-
sive, but attractive, and, I am persuaded, useful.
Some weeks ago, in reviewing Mr. Thomas Boyd’s
Poems, I took occasion to notice the want of some sort
of manual of our Irish mythology, legend, and folk-
lore. I suggested that what would serve us best would
be a Dictionary of these things, and I am still of opinion
that such a work would prove not only useful, but in-
teresting. Take things as they stand at present. Sup-
pose I suddenly want to know something about Balor,
where am I to turn for a concise and readily accessible
account of him? Suppose I want to know something of
Ollamh Fodhla, wheream I tolook him up? I certainly
do not know. I can, of course, guess certain books as
being likely to contain an account of, or, at least, a re-
ference to, these names, but how long might I spend
turning over the pages of any one of the volumes—sup-
posing I guessed the right one—before I came upon the
desiderated information? And even when I had found
it, might it not prove to be merely a passing reference,
not a satisfying account of the deity or other being re-
ferred to? Searching in a book for information, and,
thanks to the absence of an index, not knowing where
to find it, is one of the least exhilarating occupations I
know. It means a dead certain waste of some time,
some trouble, and possibly some temper, before the in-
formation tardily comes to light. Now a dictionary is
the form of work best suited to prevent this loss of time
and trouble, for there you are sure to find what you
want*in a few moments, and to get a concise, but suffi-
cient, account of it in a clear, easily apprehended form.
The form of the Dictionary I have in mind would be
that of Lempriere, whose famous Classical Dictionary

hJLS stood_ the test of time better than any other work 
of the kind ever done. 
More amoitious and erudite 
works there may be nowadays, such as the learned 
P1;el~er's work, and · the precisei and much-illustrated 
D1ct1on~ry by Dr. Smith; but for all that, Lempriere 
holds his own. 
His work is uniform lucid concise; 
writ~en with even excellence and eleg~nce throughout, 
and 1t forms a most delightful garden, peoplecl with the 
gods, heroes, and eminent mortals of what we may truly 
call "old times." We want a ·book like that for Irish 
readers. 
v'\Te want our ancient history, myth, legend, 
folk and fairy lore put into a manageable, get-at-able 
form~ and I will hazard tho opinion that were the thing 
sufficiently well done, it would find readers, and, per-
haps, inspire writers-as Lempriere certainly did John 
Keats. Just as Lempriere ta.lees in real historical per-
sons down to a certain date, so should I take in actual 
historical, literary, and other notable figures clown to, 
say, the dea.th of Sarsfield. 
In one respect, at least, I 
should improve upon the admirable Lempriere,-the too 
small fry should be omitted. 
Lempriere is amusing at 
times, when at the tail-end of an a.rticle dealing with 
some very general name~uch as " Cleon "-we find a 
number of obscure persons of" the same cognomen 
polished off a.s: " A statuary-a tyrant O'f Sicyon-a 
friend of Phocion," and so on. 
We don't learn much 
from such terribly condensed, descriptive statements as, 
say: "A person slain by Ulysses," or "A man put to 
death by Tiberius," or "A freedman of Pompey," or 
"A pa.rasite of Demetrius." The imagination fails to 
take fire at such bald pellets of ancient history, and it 
would fail equally to kindle at similar pellets, no less 
bald, of Irish history-or rather historical biography. 
.As for the matter of style, there, I think, we could 
not do better than aim at the simple, straightforward, 
yet elegant, manner of Lempriere. 
I should certainly 
hold out for simplicity and directness; I would have no 
flowers of speech, no affected archaicisms or affected re-
finements. 
Yet I would not have " journalese" either 
-the style of the On dit paragraphist, or of the " By 
the Way" man in the Freeman. 
Lempriere's conci-
sion should be followed, too ; his longest article is never 
long enough to tire one, and if he errs at all, it is on 
the spare side, not on the redundant side. 
Again, the 
balance of interests should be as fairly held as Lem-
priere holds it; with him there is no unfair preponder-
ance of Greek or of Roman, of myth or of real history. 
All are given their fair and due space in their turn. 
I 
fear some literary hands might not hold the balance of 
interests equally fair in the Irish Lempriere. 
One of 
the Yeats school might be all for fairies, and the very 
oldest and greyest gods of all; others might flood you 
with Cuchullin, or Deirdre, while others might make 
the " Island of Saints and Scholars '' element a wee 
taste too prominent--for· my taste. 
Be this, howeve,r, 
a,s it may, I cannot help thinking that if the thing 
were only sufficiently well done, it would not only b~ a 
success, but an inspiration to many a young Insh 
writer in the days to come.-

I will be told that all this is well enough understood,

FIGHTING THE BIRRELIGIOUS BILL.

HE main fact which must be realised by English
Catholics in fighting the present Education Act
is, that whether the scheme of the 1902 Act was good
or bad, just or unjust, it is quite impossible that it
should continue in force, and it is a constitutional absur-
dity to suggest that the English Parliament should
maintain it as a whole. Its continuance was in issue
at the recent General Election, and the constituencies
decided against it with no doubtful voice. It is useless,
then, to hearken back to the scheme of 1902, or to dis-
cuss its merits; whether the present Bill passes in an
amended shape, as there is strong reason to apprehend it
will, or some other measure takes its place; every man
in his senses must realise that the solution to be now
adopted must necessarily differ in essence from that
contained in the earlier Act. The only effective way of
fighting would, therefore, seem to be, that Catholics
should not remain weeping over the pleasures of the:
past, but boldly face the future, and come forward
with a definite educational compromise of their own.
Now one line of policy that might be suggested is to
seek to revert to the pre-Balfourian position, when
Catholic schools were granted unimpaired freedom.

that mtl_iet Catholics or their opponents are inclined to
“support it. Worse still, such a scheme is clearly lacking
“in finality. The continuance of a number of financially-
sbarved schools in its midst, is an anomaly which a
modern community will be slow to tolerate. I do not
think, therefore, that either the English Catholics or
the English people would have much relish for that com-
bination of denominationalism with under-endowment,
which existed in the past.

. Thoe recent Act contains, in embryo, a principle which
is capable of being developed and utilised to much
greater benefit by Catholics—namely, the principle of
real parental control. Hitherto two forms of construc-
tive or fictitious parental control have been in competi-
tion. And when the rector and other managers based
their control over the school on pavental right, the
County Councils could make a very fair case against
them, when they claimed that, being popularly elected,
they themselves were the real parental representatives.
Of. course, they, too, being elected by childless electors,
and by electors who had nothing to do with the parti-
cular school in question, were only constructive parents,
and if put in competition with the control of the real
parents, their right is evidently less.  Hence, in my
view, the Catholic policy should be to bring the schools,
as much as possible, under the direct control of the
actual parents, and not of Councils on the one hand, or
rectors, trustees, etc., on the other.

The Bill, as I have said, contains the germ of this
idea. The method in which the special provision for
Catholic and Jewish schools is sought to be made is by
permitting the granting of extended facilities, where,
after inquiry, four-fifths of the paren's are found to
desire it. Here we have the wishes of the actual, and
not the constructive or fictitious, parent taken into ac-
count. Of course, the extent of the recognition in the
present provision is ludicrously and outrageously small.
But why not extend the control of the actual parent to,
at least, a share of the management of the school, and
to the appointment of the teacher? For if you can
once have the actual parent represented, to what voice
can greater weight be attached in the education of the
child? He only requires the privilege of organisation
and representation to become all-powerful. Only when
the rights of a minority of parents have to be safe-
guarded against an intolerant majority, or when effi-
ciency of education must be secured at all cost, has any
higher power a right to step in. The two principles,
then, on which Catholics should insist are—(1) the
direct representation of the actual parent; (2) the giv-
ing authority to his representatives, especially in re-
gard to the selection of the teacher.

The actual scheme put forward by way of amend-
:ment might, I think, be something of this nature: —

(@) Tho actual parents, each having a single vote,
should elect by ballot triennially four out of six
managers for the school. The County Council
should elect the other two. The parents’ elec-
tion should be carried out by an independent
public authority.

(3) The six managers thus clected should choose
the teacher, who should not be compelled to sub-
seribe to any religious tests, or to teach religion
unless he desired it. The County Council should
have a veto on the ground of efficiency, tested by
oxamination or the like, but all existing teachers
should be conclusively decmed efficient.

(¢) The managers thus elected should decide all
questions as to the teaching, or not tezwllupg, of
any religion in a school, subject to a provision.

(1) That no public money be applied to tho teaching
of any religion (except Birreligion).

(2) That no religion (except Birreligion) hitherto un-
taught in a school should henceforward be taught in it.

(3) That no child, whose parent objects, be compelled
to attend—or put to serious inconvenience by not at-
tending—religious teaching, ; !

(¢) A meeting of all parents would be held, ab least,
once a year, to which the managers would submit
a report. B

The three provisions in brackets are illogical conces-
sions, which go rather far, but which might possibly be
made to Nonconformist prejudices. It 1s, of course,
utterly absurd that simple bible-teaching, or Bxr{elllgxoﬂ,
as it has been called, should havo exceptional privileges,
but as the granting of these privileges will placate Non-

might be politic to agree to them in the course of nego-
tiation. What Catholics neced is rather teachers than
teaching. Were the above amendment adopted, the re-
presentatives of the Catholic parents would have a
majority among the managers, and thus be able to elect
the teacher, and secure Catholic religious teaching.
which could be paid for out of the rent of the school.
It would not be necessary to compel a Catholic teacher
to teach religion. No public money is paid for religious

country, so that point is one that could easily be con-
ceded. Again, religious teaching in our National
Schools is separated from secular teaching, so that really
extended facilities for teaching it technically in school
hours is not judged, from an Irish point of view, a
very vital matter, and here also concession might, per-
haps, be made. Again, as to tests: it is not by tests
one Irish Catholic knows another, and the elected Irish
Catholic managers would be able to select suitable
teachers without the need of them.

In Protestant schools the amendment, if adopted,
would probably work differently. The Nonconformist
minority, by plumping, could obtain, at least, one mana-
gerial representative in the four, and thus, if due con-
cessions were not made to them, the decision of the
whole matter would rest ultimately with the County
Council. Of course, Catholics would be principally in-
terested in having the provision applied to their own
schools; but the advantage of taking such a line of
action is, that it would place the Catholic claim on an
entirely rational and systematic basis—namely, the
direct control of the actual parents. And parental con-
trol is, in theory, the demand of both sides. Once se-
cure direct parental representation, and any violation of
the wishes of the parental representatives becomes an in-
defensible derogation from the admitted principle of
parental control. A refusal, for instance, on the part
of a County Council to obey the desires of four-fifths of
(:he_pm‘e}]bs becomes an unwarrantable interference with
their children; the “four-fifths ”’ provision in the pre-
sent Bill, if continued at all, would have to be made
compulsory instead of permissive. ~ Exceptions might,
no doubt, at first be made in various matters to the prin-
ciple of parental control, but it would be these excep-
tions—and not, as at present, the principle itself—that
would, in time, tend to disappear. The Church of
England Rector is afraid to entrust power to the mem-
bers of his flock. The Catholic priest will have no such
fear.  And, fm_- an amendment making the actual
parent supreme, it is possible that even the support of
Labour members might be obtained.

ELECTRIC SPARKS.

News from Natal shows that Bambaata is “still in
the. bush.”  The coward! Instead of coming out—
* his breast expanding for the ball ’—he remains where
the Unmvoti Rifles can’t get a fair shot at him. Is he
aman at all?

Mr. Puck thinks that the man who won’t come out
and be shot—when the interests of “civilisation '’
plainly require that he should be shot—is no man at all.

In a lyric in the Weekly Freeman, we find these lines :

“Do be glad, my dearest, sweetest,
Brighter days will come to you.”

Now, who says poetry is dead in Ireland? We love
that expression, “ Do be glad.” Tt is most sweeb!

In another lyric in the same magazine of literature,
we are told of a young lady that:

“ Her voice excels a chime of bells,
When sweetly they are ringing.”’

That must be a most unusual voice, entirely! The
young lady could be used as a church bell any day.

The Woman’s Suffrage scene in the House of Com-
mons was great. A leading lady suffragist, while being
removed, declared: “It’s no use talking; we shall have
to do something to draw attention to our rights—com-
mit a murder, for instance.”” We hope no such ex-
treme will ever be reached, but if it should, we hope a
Bung may be selected as an acceptable victim for the
female fury. .

There is a Mr. Denison Faber in Parliament who
seems to be somewhat of a Mr. Puck. In the discussion
on the vote for the Lord President’s salary, he said:
“ One anomaly leads to another. You appoint a Lord
President, who has nothing to do, and then you give
him a Private Secretary to do it.”” No wonder they
laughed—especially as the salary of the Private Secre-
tary is being increased !

By the way, the Lord President gets £2,000 a year
for having “ nothing to do,’”” and the present man, Lord
Crewe, is said to be enjoying a rental of £40,000 a
year! But the £2,000 must come in handy, too, for it
would easily cover his lordship’s ’bus fare to and from
his office. Next to being Lord Mayor of Dublin, the
best thing going is this Lord Presidentship.

A noted man of science, Sir William Flower, is said
to have expressed regret that only the brains of paupers
and lunatics are available for dissection. It is too bad,
certainly. How if a few Bungs and bona’s were thrown
in? Would their “grey matter’’ be of any use? Or
the cerebral tissues of a few M.P.’s? Why might not
those members of the late Cabinet, who were defeated
at the polls, be put to death, and their brains sent to
the anatomist and physiologist?

In a speech to his Advisory Committee at Cork, Mr.
William O’Brien says he has been holding out “‘the
olive branch ”’ in every speech he made this ever so long.
Faith, most of us thought it was a cat-o’-nine-tails he
held out, but see, now, how a good man may be mis-
taken !

Mr. Puck read, with acute attention, the financial
Report, which Provost Tony Traill read at the recent
meeting of the (“I. C.”) General Synod. As the
assets of the “Church of Ireland” tot wup to
£8,650,973 9s. 4d., there seems no immediate danger
of starvation setting in among that body. Under these

circumstances, and the fact that the aforesaid Church
has an annual income from investments of some
£395,172, Mr. Puck relinquishes his project of starting
a fund in aid of emaciated C. I. clergymen.

have sufficed to meet the case. A levy of £3 per parish
off the 1,090 parishes would have put the hierarchy

That is, of course, if the levy per parish instead of cover-
ing the net premiums of the Church Company had ap-
proximated to the gross.

What these premiums for re-insurances amount to,
the public has never been told; nor is the subject one
respecting which the half-dozen shareholders, who at-

themselves to the building of churches, and religious

ments, during the last sixty or eighty years, has been
greatly exaggerated. It is to be hoped, however, that
the sums still paid to English offices for Irish eccle-
siastical insurances are as largo as rumoured—as in the
event of an insurance office being established by some
section of the Catholic laity, a fairly good slice of these
substantial re-insurances would surely be given to them,-
even although Mr. Jorkins might be expected to object.

including the “considerable’” one from Clonliffe Colleée
only aggregated £89, the terms in which the payment of
them are narrated would appear to be just a little bom-

Speaking for myself, I must say that as soon as I
began to understand spoken Irish, such passages as the
abovo, especially when they occurred in comic plays
(there is such a passage, I think, in “Seaghan na Scuab’’)
struck me as if some incongruity had happened—of
course the incongruity was between my mind and that
of the dramatist. These little books, being published
at present in Irish, contain many lessons for us; for one
thing, they show the lack of the whole of Irishism in
Anglo-Irish literature.

Now, whereto all this? Well, if the reader has not
caught up the whereto before now, it will not be caught
up at all this time. Would it not be well for all our
writers at least to remember the fact that when a Catho-
lic Irishman writes for an English magazine he %as to
keep his audience in mind? Would it not mean pro-
gress towards wnationhood if we got the converse
of this to hold true? Canon Sheehan was
perhaps the first of our modern writers to point out
how much backbone was wanting to Catholic Ireland
as regards the expression of their religious feel-
ings—would it not be well if Catholic Ireland began
thinking whether that want does not interfere with the
realising of an Irish nation? Lastly, is it not time for
all to recognise the fact, that “ Church and State are
one and the same thing, being different integral parts
